one_line_takeaway: A CSIRO Data61/U. Ottawa systematic literature review of 112 primary studies produces a taxonomy, reusable template meta-model, and four-pattern library for constructing AI safety cases (assurance documentation) -- this is an SE/governance-methodology contribution, not a security attack, defense, or vulnerability study.
key_finding: The literature-coding step (Section 3.6.2) identifies three recurring structural weaknesses across the 112 reviewed AI safety-case studies: (1) acceptability criteria are underspecified -- comparative/marginal claims frequently invoke a 'no worse than' baseline without formalizing the comparator's own assurance status or a failure budget/threshold; (2) evidence quality criteria and traceability are inconsistently stated -- red-teaming/stress-testing/audit artefacts are common but independence, coverage, recency, and reproducibility of that evidence are rarely specified; (3) post-deployment assurance is fragmented -- only a minority of studies (reported as ≤ 10%) link safety claims to operational monitoring/governance actions despite AI systems changing post-deployment. These three gaps are used to justify the design requirements for the paper's proposed templates (mandatory comparator/threshold declaration, evidence quality criteria, and update triggers).
core_contribution: The paper (Lee, Zhu, Shamsujjoha, Dong, Lu, Chen, Briand; CSIRO Data61 / U. Ottawa) reports a Systematic Literature Review (SLR) of AI safety-case construction and, from it, derives: (1) an AI-specific Claims-Arguments-Evidence (CAE) taxonomy (3 claim types: assertion-based, constrained-based, capability-limited, each with sub-types; 6 argument types: demonstrative, comparative, risk-based, causal/explanatory, capability-oriented, normative/conformance, each mapped to a reasoning logic -- deductive, inductive, abductive, statistical, analogical; 7 evidence families: empirical, comparative, model-based, expert-derived, formal methods, operational/field data, mechanistic), (2) a reusable safety-case template meta-model that instantiates these CAE elements as composable claim-argument-evidence chains, and (3) a pattern library of four end-to-end patterns (discovery-driven, marginal-risk, continuous-evolution, threshold-comparator) addressing recurring AI assurance problems such as evaluation without ground truth and dynamic model updates. The paper states it validates the framework with a case study of an AI-based government tender-evaluation system (Section 6), which falls outside the first 22 pages of extracted text and was not directly reviewed here.
threat_model: None. This is a software/systems-engineering assurance-methodology paper, not a security or adversarial-robustness study. It has no attacker model, no attack surface, and no exploit/defense evaluation. It occasionally references AI-safety-adjacent hazards (prompt injection, role-play exploitation, data exfiltration, cyber-inability capability limits) only as illustrative examples of AI system risks that a safety case's claims might need to cover -- these are drawn from cited prior work (e.g., Buhl et al.'s frontier-AI safety case, Goemans et al.'s cyber-inability template), not proposed, tested, or analyzed by this paper itself.
limitations: Within the reviewed text: the CAE taxonomy is explicitly non-exclusive/overlapping by the authors' own admission (categories are not disjoint, e.g., guideline-based and dependability arguments appear under multiple headings), which the authors frame as intentional but which also weakens its use as a strict classification tool. The 'Inferred mapping' column of Table 5 extends coverage to studies that were not retrieved through the SLR's own automated search/snowballing and that do not explicitly use CAE terminology, introducing an authors-acknowledged interpretive-bias risk in the taxonomy-to-literature mapping. The review's corpus cutoff is August 2025, so newer work is excluded by construction. The validation of the proposed templates/patterns rests on a single illustrative case study (a government tender-evaluation AI system) rather than multiple independent deployments or a controlled comparison against practitioners not using the framework.

datasets_used: Corpus of 112 primary studies selected via the SLR pipeline (search + snowballing + quality filtering) as the analysis corpus; no ML training/eval dataset is used since this is a literature-review paper
quantitative_results: 1,235 papers returned by the initial automated search across IEEE Xplore (119), ACM Digital Library (904), ScienceDirect (83), SpringerLink (129)
807 papers returned via forward/backward snowballing from 9 seed papers
1,070 additional papers returned via backward snowballing (Google Scholar) on the 41 database-selected papers
264 papers removed as duplicates/editorials/no-author records (205 from database search, 59 from snowballing)
112 primary studies retained after cross-check and merge for full data extraction/synthesis

QC scores for the 112 selected studies ranged 2.73-4.55 (1-5 scale), mean 3.37; 96/112 (~86%) scored >=3.0, including 22/112 (~20%) scoring >4.0; 16/112 (~14%) scored 2.0-3.0 and were retained after team discussion
11 studies of 123 post-merge candidates excluded for QC score below the 2.0 threshold
Article-type distribution of the 112 selected studies: journal article 40 (35.71%), arXiv preprint 35 (31.25%), conference paper 31 (27.67%), PhD thesis 4, technical report 2
Publication-year distribution of the 112 selected studies: ~2020: 29, 2021: 4, 2022: 11, 2023: 10, 2024: 29, 2025: 29
Post-deployment/operational-monitoring linkage found in <=10% of the 112 reviewed studies, per the authors' own coding
baselines_compared: Not an empirical/system-performance paper, so no algorithmic baselines are benchmarked. The paper instead positions its taxonomy/template/pattern contribution against four representative prior approaches in its own Table 1: Buhl et al.'s frontier-AI safety-case guidance, Goemans et al.'s cyber-inability CAE template, Cârlan et al.'s Dynamic Safety-Case Management System (DSCMS)/Checkable Safety Arguments, and Clymer et al.'s LLM-misuse-safeguard safety-case sketch
